critic_raw:
The design uses a stratified random split justified by the claim that 'temporal autocorrelation in patient outcomes is not significant enough to necessitate a purely chronological split.' This claim is empirically false for 30-day hospital readmission prediction. Readmission rates exhibit well-documented temporal structure: hospital quality improvement initiatives reduce readmission rates over multi-year observation windows, seasonal illness patterns (flu season, heat waves) affect short-term readmission risk, and ICD-10 coding changes in 2015 introduced discontinuities in diagnosis code distributions. A random split drawn from a three-year window will mix pre- and post-coding-change records, pre- and post-quality-improvement-initiative records, and seasonal effects — allowing the model to implicitly smooth over regime boundaries that would be novel at deployment time.

Second, the design specifies recall as the primary metric because stakeholders want to 'minimize false negatives and better capture high-risk patients.' But the model selection criterion is recall on the validation set — which, as noted for eval_scenario_791, selects the most permissive threshold, not the most discriminative model. Any model architecture trained with appropriate loss weighting will approach recall close to 1.0 by predicting all patients as high-risk. The design does not specify a precision floor or a threshold selection procedure tied to operational capacity constraints.

Third, the RNN's features include 'time-stamped sequences of diagnosis codes, treatment history, and follow-up visits.' Follow-up visits after discharge directly reflect readmission risk — a patient with a scheduled follow-up visit 5 days post-discharge has lower unplanned readmission risk than one with no follow-up scheduled. If follow-up visit records that are scheduled at discharge time are included as features, this is prospectively valid. But if follow-up visit records that are completed after discharge appear in the feature set at the time of prediction, this constitutes post-event leakage: the model knows whether the patient actually attended the follow-up before predicting readmission, which is an outcome proxy.

Fourth, the logistic regression baseline is described as 'fine-tuned using grid search to secure a fair comparison.' For a logistic regression on demographic and clinical variables, 'fine-tuning' typically means regularization strength selection. This is a single hyperparameter with well-understood behavior. Calling this equivalent to tuning an RNN's architecture (depth, hidden size, dropout, learning rate, sequence length, padding strategy) overstates the comparability of tuning efforts and may obscure underfitting of the logistic regression baseline.
all_issues_raised: The claim of weak temporal autocorrelation over a three-year readmission window is empirically contestable: ICD-10 coding changes, seasonal illness cycles, and hospital quality improvement initiatives introduce regime shifts that a random split conceals but a temporal split would expose.

Recall as primary model selection criterion without a precision floor selects maximally permissive threshold configurations rather than clinically useful discriminative models; the design does not specify a threshold selection procedure tied to operational capacity constraints.

Follow-up visit records that are completed post-discharge may appear in the RNN's feature set as prospectively knowable information, but if they are recorded after the readmission outcome window begins, including them constitutes post-event leakage through outcome-correlated proxy features.

Grid search over regularization strength for logistic regression is not comparable in scope to RNN hyperparameter search (depth, width, dropout, learning rate, sequence length), understating the potential for logistic regression underfitting and overstating the fairness of the tuning comparison.
all_issues_adjudicated: The claim of weak temporal autocorrelation over a three-year readmission window is empirically contestable: ICD-10 coding changes, seasonal illness cycles, and hospital quality improvement initiatives introduce regime shifts that a random split conceals but a temporal split would expose.

Recall as primary model selection criterion without a precision floor selects maximally permissive threshold configurations rather than clinically useful discriminative models; the design does not specify a threshold selection procedure tied to operational capacity constraints.

Follow-up visit records that are completed post-discharge may appear in the RNN's feature set as prospectively knowable information, but if they are recorded after the readmission outcome window begins, including them constitutes post-event leakage through outcome-correlated proxy features.

Grid search over regularization strength for logistic regression is not comparable in scope to RNN hyperparameter search (depth, width, dropout, learning rate, sequence length), understating the potential for logistic regression underfitting and overstating the fairness of the tuning comparison.
